q: What problem was Nginx designed to solve, and how?
a: The C10k problem (10,000+ concurrent connections). It uses an event-driven, asynchronous model — a few worker processes each running an event loop — instead of one thread/process per connection.

q: Why does an event loop handle far more concurrent connections than thread-per-connection?
a: Most of a connection's life is spent waiting (network, slow clients, backends). An event loop only does work when a connection is ready, so a few workers juggle tens of thousands of mostly-idle connections cheaply.

q: What's the difference between the Nginx master process and worker processes?
a: The master (root) reads config and binds privileged ports but handles no traffic; workers (unprivileged, ~one per CPU core) do all request handling. This split enables zero-downtime reloads.

q: What is the Nginx config context hierarchy?
a: main → events / http → server → location. Outer settings are inherited by inner contexts unless overridden.

q: How does Nginx pick which `server` block handles a request?
a: By the `listen` port plus a matching `server_name` (against the Host header). Non-matching requests go to the default_server (or the first block on that port).

q: In location matching, what's the precedence and the surprising rule about regex?
a: Exact `=` wins first; then longest prefix (`^~` stops further checks); then regex (`~`/`~*`) — and the FIRST matching regex wins, so order matters; otherwise the longest plain prefix.

q: What's the difference between `root` and `alias`?
a: `root` APPENDS the full URI to the path; `alias` REPLACES the matched location prefix. Mixing them up is the most common static-file 404.

q: What two commands form the safe edit→reload workflow?
a: `sudo nginx -t` (test config syntax/validity) then `sudo nginx -s reload` (graceful, zero-downtime reload). Always test before reloading.

q: What does `try_files $uri $uri/ =404;` do?
a: Tries to serve the requested file, then the request as a directory (its index), and if neither exists returns 404. It's the workhorse of static serving.

q: What single line makes a single-page app (SPA) work in Nginx?
a: try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html; — real files are served directly, and any unmatched route falls back to index.html so the client-side router can handle it.

q: How should you cache hashed assets vs. index.html, and why?
a: Hashed/fingerprinted assets: cache immutable for ~1 year (the hash changes on content change). index.html: no-cache, so deploys take effect and don't reference deleted assets.

q: What's the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
a: A forward proxy sits in front of clients and represents them to the internet (hides the client). A reverse proxy (Nginx) sits in front of servers and represents them to clients (hides the backend).

q: Name three reasons to put Nginx as a reverse proxy in front of an app.
a: TLS termination, load balancing, caching/compression, rate limiting, a stable public surface during redeploys, serving static files, and shielding the app from direct exposure.

q: Which headers should a reverse proxy forward to the backend, and which prevents redirect loops?
a: Host, X-Real-IP, X-Forwarded-For, and X-Forwarded-Proto. X-Forwarded-Proto (the original http/https) is what prevents infinite HTTPS redirect loops.

q: What's the `proxy_pass` trailing-slash rule?
a: If proxy_pass ends with a path/slash, Nginx REPLACES the matched location prefix; with no path it passes the URI through unchanged. Mismatches cause backend 404s.

q: What extra directives does proxying WebSockets require?
a: proxy_http_version 1.1; plus proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; and proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade"; (and a long proxy_read_timeout).

q: Compare round-robin, least_conn, and ip_hash load balancing.
a: round-robin (default): even rotation. least_conn: send to the least-busy backend (good for uneven durations). ip_hash: pin each client IP to one backend (sticky sessions). Better long-term: make the app stateless.

q: How does open-source Nginx do passive health checks?
a: With `max_fails` and `fail_timeout` on an upstream server, e.g. `max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s` — after 3 failures in 30s the backend is ejected for 30s. (Active probing needs Nginx Plus.)

q: In Nginx HTTPS config, what must `ssl_certificate` point to and why?
a: The FULL chain (leaf cert + intermediates), not just the leaf — otherwise some clients can't build a trust path even though browsers (which cache intermediates) may work. (PKI chain-of-trust.)

q: How do you redirect HTTP to HTTPS in Nginx?
a: A port-80 server block with: return 301 https://$host$request_uri; — a cheap permanent redirect that preserves the path.

q: What does enabling `ssl_stapling` accomplish?
a: OCSP stapling: Nginx fetches a signed revocation-status proof and staples it into the handshake, so visitors don't query the CA themselves (faster, no privacy leak). It's the PKI OCSP-stapling concept.

q: What does HSTS do, and what does it protect against?
a: Strict-Transport-Security tells browsers to use only HTTPS for your domain for a set duration, closing the gap where a first plain-http request could be hijacked before the redirect.

q: How do most sites obtain and renew TLS certs automatically?
a: certbot (or acme.sh) via the ACME protocol / Let's Encrypt: it proves domain control, installs a free cert into the Nginx config, and auto-renews unattended (with a zero-downtime reload).

q: What does proxy caching add over browser caching, and what's `proxy_cache_use_stale`?
a: Proxy caching stores backend responses so a slow endpoint is computed once and served many times by Nginx. proxy_cache_use_stale serves the last good cached response during a backend error/timeout — staying up through an outage.

q: What governs Nginx's maximum concurrent connections?
a: Roughly worker_processes × worker_connections. With `worker_processes auto` (one per core) and a few thousand worker_connections, one box holds tens of thousands of connections.

q: In `limit_req zone=api burst=20 nodelay;`, what do rate/burst/nodelay control?
a: rate (in the zone, e.g. 10r/s) is the sustained allowed rate; burst absorbs short spikes (queued requests); nodelay serves the burst immediately instead of queuing it.

q: Why might a security header set in an outer block disappear when you add a header in a `location`?
a: `add_header` is NOT inherited into an inner block that defines any of its own add_header directives — the inner block must repeat the ones it still wants.

q: Which log variable tells you whether slowness is your backend or Nginx?
a: $upstream_response_time (vs $request_time). Add it to a custom log_format — if upstream time is high, the backend is slow, not the proxy.

q: List three quick Nginx hardening steps.
a: server_tokens off (hide version); block dotfiles (location ~ /\. { deny all; }); client_max_body_size limit; client/header timeouts; run workers unprivileged.
